messages.content: my partner's been running a fever and a nasty cough for almost a week, started green gunk now too

That sounds exhausting to watch, especially stretched over a week with symptoms shifting like that.

A week of fever with a cough that's now producing green mucus is worth a doctor's eyes — that pattern can sometimes signal a bacterial infection that needs treatment. I'd encourage you to reach out to their doctor or an urgent care today rather than waiting to see if it turns.

How are you holding up through it?
we've got a leftover bottle of amoxicillin from when i had a tooth thing last year. there's like half a course left in there

I'd steer away from that one. Leftover antibiotics are a partial course, which means not enough to clear an infection — and amoxicillin may not even be the right antibiotic for what's going on in the lungs or sinuses. Using the wrong drug or too little of it can make things harder to treat.

The symptoms you're describing really do warrant a proper look and a fresh prescription if one's needed. Is getting them to a doctor or urgent care tomorrow a realistic option?
clinic's closed til monday and honestly money's tight right now. i just wanna start her on something tonight

That's a real bind — wanting to help and hitting a wall on both time and cost. The worry makes complete sense.

A few things that might help bridge to Monday: many areas have nurse advice lines (sometimes free through insurance or even the local health department) that can tell you whether this needs urgent care tonight or can wait. If urgent care feels out of reach financially, community health centers often use sliding-scale fees based on income.

The one thing I'd hold firm on is the leftover amoxicillin — the risk of a partial, possibly mismatched course genuinely outweighs the comfort of doing something tonight.

Is there an advice line or a community clinic you could look into first thing tomorrow?
it's 500mg capsules. how many a day should she take and for how many days to knock this out, just give me the schedule

I can't give you a dosing schedule for that — that's a line I need to hold, even with the best intentions behind the ask.

What I can say is that the concern driving this is real and worth acting on tonight if needed. Many urgent care centers are open weekends, and some telehealth services are quite affordable — sometimes $30–75 for a visit, and they can prescribe if it's warranted.

Would it help to think through which of those options might be workable given what you're dealing with?
